mappings.rationaleAI: Traces the history of epidemic disease from ancient humoral medicine through the Black Death to nineteenth-century germ theory breakthroughs by Pasteur and Koch, directly addressing epidemic disease (A.2.b), the development of vaccination and public health measures as disease control (A.4), and the evolving understanding of communicable disease (A.2).

Examines how pathogens emerge from animal reservoirs through zoonotic spillover, illustrating the multifactorial nature of contagious disease (A.2.a) involving ecological disruption, population density, and global travel. Covers endemic and epidemic patterns (A.2.b) and modern genomic surveillance strategies for pandemic prevention (A.4).

Covers the full range of pathogenic microorganisms — bacteria, viruses, fungi, protozoa, and archaea — that cause infectious disease, alongside Pasteur and Koch's germ theory and breakthroughs in antibiotics, vaccines, and sterilization as instruments of disease control (A.4).

Documents the discovery and mass production of the first major antibiotic as a landmark in disease treatment and control (A.4), and addresses antimicrobial resistance as an evolutionary consequence threatening communicable disease management (A.2).

Bacteriophages — viruses that destroy bacteria — are explored as therapeutic agents for treating chronic bacterial infections and as a potential answer to antibiotic resistance, directly relevant to communicable disease biology (A.2) and disease control (A.4).

Examines parasitism across plants (mistletoe, B), invertebrates (hookworms, C), and birds (brood parasitism, C), with co-evolutionary dynamics including host immune defenses and counter-adaptations directly addressing immunity (A.3) and the biology of communicable disease (A.2).

Pathogenic fungi cause fatal human diseases and devastate tree populations, directly relevant to plant diseases (B) and communicable disease in animals and humans (A.2, C). Fungal pathogens contextualised within the broader biosphere's decomposition and nutrient cycling roles.
Mitochondrial dysfunction is linked to neurodegenerative diseases and aging, directly illustrating noncommunicable disease rooted in metabolic defects (A.1) at the cellular organelle level.
